rules.title: Locative Suffix: -de / -da / -te / -ta
rules.explanation: The locative suffix shows location (at, in, on). It follows vowel harmony: use -de after front vowels (e, i, ö, ü) and -da after back vowels (a, ı, o, u). Also, after voiceless consonants (p, ç, t, k, ş, s, h, f), the suffix starts with -t instead of -d (te/ta). A common mistake is forgetting to change d to t or applying wrong vowel harmony.

examples.note: Because 'p' is voiceless, the suffix becomes -ta, not -da.
Vowel harmony: 'u' is a back vowel, so suffix must be -da, not -de.
'ş' is voiceless, so suffix becomes -te.

exercises.prompt: Write the correct Turkish form of 'kitap' with the locative suffix (in the book).
Write the correct Turkish form of 'okul' with the locative suffix (at school).
Write the correct Turkish form of 'iş' with the locative suffix (at work).

rules.title: Present Continuous vs. Present Simple (Şimdiki Zaman vs. Geniş Zaman)
rules.explanation: In Turkish, use the present continuous tense (-iyor) for actions happening right now or around the moment of speaking. Use the present simple tense (-r, -ar, -er) for habitual actions, routines, and general truths. A common mistake is mixing these: using the present simple for ongoing actions or the present continuous for habits.

examples.note: Use present continuous for actions in progress at the moment of speaking.
Use present simple for daily routines and habits.

exercises.prompt: Translate into Turkish: 'I am reading a book now.'
Translate into Turkish: 'I drink tea every morning.'

rules.title: Using 'için' with Pronouns
rules.explanation: In Turkish, the postposition 'için' (for) requires the preceding noun or pronoun to be in the genitive case. For personal pronouns, you must use the genitive forms: benim, senin, onun, bizim, sizin, onların. Using the nominative (ben, sen, etc.) is a common mistake made by English speakers.

examples.note: Personal pronouns require genitive case before 'için'.
Same rule applies for 'sen'.
'O' becomes 'onun' before 'için'.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the blank: _____ (I) için kitap aldı.
Fill in the blank: _____ (you - informal) için yemek yaptım.
Fill in the blank: _____ (he/she) için geldi.

rules.title: Possessive Suffixes (İyelik Ekleri) in Turkish
rules.explanation: In Turkish, when you say 'my,' 'your,' or 'his/her,' you must add a possessive suffix to the noun. A common mistake is to forget this suffix and just use the pronoun alone. The suffix changes based on the person (ben, sen, o) and vowel harmony.

examples.note: The suffix -m (with vowel harmony) is added to 'araba' for 'my car'.
For 'your house,' add -in to 'ev' (because ev ends in a consonant, use -in).
For 'his/her book,' use -ı (and note the consonant mutation: p becomes b).

exercises.prompt: Write 'my school' in Turkish.
Write 'your pencil' in Turkish (singular informal).
Write 'his/her garden' in Turkish.

rules.title: Vowel Harmony (Ünlü Uyumu)
rules.explanation: Turkish vowel harmony means that vowels in suffixes must match the last vowel of the root word based on two features: front/back (e, i, ö, ü vs. a, ı, o, u) and rounded/unrounded. For beginners, focus on the major rule: if the last vowel is a back vowel (a, ı, o, u), the suffix vowel must also be back; if it is a front vowel (e, i, ö, ü), the suffix vowel must be front. This applies to common suffixes like plural -ler/-lar, locative -de/-da, and ablative -den/-dan.

examples.note: Kitap has back vowel 'a', so the plural suffix must be -lar, not -ler.
Ev has front vowel 'e', so the locative suffix must be -de, not -da.
Okul has back vowel 'u', so the locative suffix must be -da, not -de.

exercises.prompt: Make the plural of 'kitap' (book).
Make the locative of 'okul' (school).
Make the ablative of 'ev' (house).

rules.title: Common Turkish Stress (Vurgu) Mistake
rules.explanation: In Turkish, the stress (vurgu) typically falls on the last syllable of a word. English speakers often mistakenly stress the first syllable, which can make the word sound unnatural. Remembering to stress the final syllable will improve your pronunciation.

examples.note: The word 'kitap' (book) should be stressed on the last syllable 'tap'.
The word 'okul' (school) should be stressed on the last syllable 'kul'.
The word 'güzel' (beautiful) should be stressed on the last syllable 'zel'.

exercises.prompt: How would you correctly pronounce 'elma' (apple) in terms of stress?
What is the correct stress for 'araba' (car)?
Correct the stress: 'PEncere' (window) – wrong stress on first syllable.

rules.title: Turkish Intonation (Tonlama) in Questions
rules.explanation: In Turkish, intonation patterns differ from English for questions. Yes/no questions require the suffix -mi (with vowel harmony) and a rising tone on the suffix. Wh-questions (using words like ne, nerede, kim) use a falling or steady tone at the end, similar to statements. Using English rising intonation on all questions can make your speech sound unnatural.

examples.note: Always use the question suffix -mi for yes/no questions; intonation alone is not enough.
Wh-questions keep a flat or falling intonation, not rising.

exercises.prompt: Say 'Is this a pen?' in Turkish with correct question suffix and intonation.
Say 'Where do you live?' in Turkish with correct intonation.
Ask 'Are they coming?' in Turkish.

rules.title: Turkish Word Order: Subject-Object-Verb (SOV)
rules.explanation: In Turkish, the verb always comes at the end of a sentence, unlike English where it follows the subject directly. The correct order is Subject - Object - Verb (SOV). A common mistake English speakers make is placing the verb before the object (SVO), which sounds unnatural in Turkish.

examples.note: Wrong: verb 'yerim' placed after subject. Right: object 'elma' comes before verb.
Wrong: verb 'okuyor' before object. Right: object 'kitap' before verb.
Wrong: verb 'içiyor' before object. Right: object 'su' before verb.

exercises.prompt: Translate: 'I drink tea.' (Use 'ben' and 'çay')
Translate: 'She writes a letter.' (Use 'o' and 'mektup')
Translate: 'We watch a movie.' (Use 'biz' and 'film')

rules.title: Evidential Past Tense (-miş)
rules.explanation: Turkish uses the evidential past tense (-miş) to describe events you didn't witness firsthand—things you heard, inferred, or learned indirectly. English has no such distinction, so learners often mistakenly use the definite past (-di) for all past actions. Remember: if you didn’t see it happen, use -miş.

examples.note: If you didn't see Ali arrive but heard about it, use -miş, not -di.
When you taste the food and infer it was made well, use -miş for the inferred past.
If someone told you Ahmet is sick, use -miş to indicate reported information.

exercises.prompt: You see your friend's shoes are wet. Infer that it rained. (use -miş)
Your mother tells you that your brother came home. Report this.
You find out that the movie started late (hearsay).

rules.title: Accusative Case Marking in Turkish
rules.explanation: In Turkish, direct objects that are definite (specific or known) must take the accusative suffix -(y)İ, where İ changes according to vowel harmony (ı, i, u, ü). A common mistake is omitting this suffix when the object is definite, especially in sentences like 'I see the book' instead of 'I see the book' with the suffix. Remember: if the object is a specific person, thing, or includes a possessive or demonstrative, you need accusative.

examples.note: 'Kitap' (book) is indefinite here; but if you mean a specific book, use accusative 'kitabı'. The example shows the common error of omitting the suffix for a definite object.
Without accusative, 'elma' could mean 'an apple' (indefinite). With 'elmayı', it's the specific apple.
If you saw a particular car (e.g., your neighbor's car), you need the accusative suffix. Without it, it could mean 'I saw a car' (any car).

exercises.prompt: Translate 'I read the newspaper' into Turkish, using accusative case.
Correct this sentence: 'O, kalem alacak.' (He/She will buy the pen.)
Fill in the blank with the correct form: 'Ben ______ (araba) sürüyorum.' (I am driving the car.)

rules.title: Plural After Numbers in Turkish
rules.explanation: In Turkish, when you use a number with a noun, the noun must stay in its singular form. You do not add a plural suffix like -ler or -lar. This is different from English, where we say 'five books' with a plural noun. Think of the number as already indicating quantity.

examples.note: Say 'three apples' as 'üç elma', not 'üç elmalar'.
For 'ten students', use the singular 'öğrenci' after the number.

exercises.prompt: How do you say 'two cats' in Turkish? Use the singular noun.
Write 'seven books' correctly in Turkish.

rules.title: Avoid Overusing Possessive Pronouns
rules.explanation: In Turkish, possessive suffixes attached to nouns already indicate ownership. Adding separate possessive pronouns (e.g., benim, senin) before the noun is often redundant and sounds unnatural. Use them only for emphasis, contrast, or clarity, not as a default.

examples.note: The suffix -am in 'arabam' already means 'my car'. Adding 'benim' is unnecessary unless you want to stress 'my car' vs. someone else's.
'Kitabın' means 'your book'. The pronoun 'senin' is redundant in everyday questions.
'Evi' (his/her house) already contains the ownership. Only add 'onun' if you need to clarify who you mean.

exercises.prompt: Translate 'My pencil is on the table.' without using an extra possessive pronoun.
Correct the mistake: 'Benim annem çok iyi.'
How would you say 'Your bag is beautiful' in a natural way?

rules.title: Genitive-Possessive Construction
rules.explanation: In Turkish, when showing possession, the possessor takes a genitive suffix (-ın/in/un/ün) and the possessed noun takes a possessive suffix ( -(s)ı/i/u/ü, etc.). A common mistake is to add only the genitive suffix and forget the possessive suffix on the owned item.

examples.note: After the genitive possessor, the owned noun must get a possessive suffix.
The same rule applies: 'woman's bag' needs both suffixes.
Drop the final consonant? No, 'defter' becomes 'defteri' (adding -i).

exercises.prompt: Write the correct possessive form: 'My school'
Write the correct possessive form: 'Ali's book'
Write the correct possessive form: 'The girl's cat'

rules.title: Var/Yok with Possession: Don't Forget the Suffix!
rules.explanation: In Turkish, to express possession using 'var' (there is) or 'yok' (there is not), the possessed noun must carry a possessive suffix that matches the owner. Simply saying 'Benim kitap var' is incorrect because the noun lacks the suffix. Always add the appropriate possessive ending: e.g., 'Benim kitabım var' (I have a book).

examples.note: Add the possessive suffix -ım for first person singular (ben).
Add -in for second person singular (sen).
Add -i for third person singular (o).

exercises.prompt: Correct the mistake: 'Benim araba var.'
Correct the mistake: 'Onun bilgisayar yok.'

rules.title: Causative Verb Forms: Making Someone Do Something
rules.explanation: In Turkish, to express that you cause someone else to do an action, you add -dır/-dir/-dur/-dür or -tır/-tir/-tur/-tür to the verb stem. A common mistake is using the simple verb (e.g., 'yapmak') instead of the causative form (e.g., 'yaptırmak') when the subject is not the doer.

examples.note: The first says 'I washed the car' (I did it myself); the second says 'I had the car washed' (someone else did it).
The wrong version means 'He wrote the letter'; the right version means 'He had the letter written (by someone else).'

exercises.prompt: How do you say 'I made the children eat' (the children are eating because you caused them)?
Translate: 'She had the room cleaned (by someone).'

rules.title: Turkish Passive Voice: Common Mistakes
rules.explanation: In Turkish, passive voice is formed by adding suffixes like -il, -in, or -n to the verb stem. A common mistake is using the wrong suffix, especially with verbs ending in vowels or the letter 'l'. For vowel-ending stems, use -n; for verbs ending in 'l', use -in instead of -il.

examples.note: 'Oku' ends with a vowel, so the passive suffix is -n, not -il.
Verbs ending in 'l' take the suffix -in, not -il (e.g., al- + -in = alın).

exercises.prompt: Correct the passive form: 'Mektup yazıldı.' (Is it correct? If not, fix it.)
exercises.answer: Mektup yazıldı. (Correct as is; no change needed.)

exercises.prompt: Fix the passive mistake: 'Yemek pişirildi.' (The verb 'pişir' ends with 'r'. What should it be?)
exercises.answer: Yemek pişirildi. (Correct; the suffix -il is used for stems ending in consonants other than 'l'.)

rules.title: Negation with değil vs -ma
rules.explanation: Use 'değil' to negate nouns, adjectives, or locations (e.g., 'O bir öğretmen değil' = 'He is not a teacher'). Use the suffix '-ma/-me' to negate verbs (e.g., 'Gelmiyorum' = 'I am not coming'). Do not mix them: 'değil' never attaches to verbs, and '-ma/-me' is only for verbs.

examples.note: For verbs, use -ma/-me on the verb itself, not 'değil'.
'değil' is correct for adjectives; just remove the question form for a statement.
For nouns, use 'değil' + personal suffix; avoid adding extra question endings in a statement.

exercises.prompt: Translate: 'He is not a doctor.'
Translate: 'I am not going.'
Translate: 'This is not big.'

rules.title: Dative, Locative, and Ablative Cases in Turkish
rules.explanation: Turkish uses three location-related cases: dative (-e/-a, meaning 'to' or 'towards'), locative (-de/-da, meaning 'in', 'on', 'at'), and ablative (-den/-dan, meaning 'from' or 'out of'). A common mistake is using the wrong suffix—for example, saying 'evde gidiyorum' (locative) instead of 'eve gidiyorum' (dative) when saying 'I am going home'. Remember: use dative for direction, locative for position, and ablative for origin.

examples.note: For expressing direction (going to), use dative case -e/-a, not locative -de/-da.
Use locative case -de/-da to indicate where something is located (on the table), not ablative -den/-dan.
This is actually correct! Ablative is used for coming from somewhere. Just a reminder not to mix with dative/locative.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the blank with the correct case suffix: 'Ben İstanbul___ yaşıyorum.' (I live in Istanbul.)
Fill in the blank: 'Yarın Ankara___ gideceğiz.' (We will go to Ankara tomorrow.)
Fill in the blank: 'Ali ev___ çıktı.' (Ali left the house.)

rules.title: Ile vs Ve: When to Use Which
rules.explanation: In Turkish, 've' means 'and' and is used to list separate items or actions. 'ile' (or its suffix form -(y)le) means 'with' and connects two nouns that are together in an action. A common mistake is using 've' where 'ile' is needed to show accompaniment (e.g., 'I went with my friend'). Remember: if the two nouns are doing something together, use 'ile'; if you're just listing them, use 've'.

examples.note: If the meaning is 'Ali came together with Ayşe', use 'ile' (attached as -'yle) instead of 've'.
When you and someone else do an action together, attach 'ile' to the second noun (arkadaşım + la).

exercises.prompt: Translate: 'I went to the park with my sister.'
Fill in the blank with 've' or 'ile' (attached appropriately): 'Ahmet ____ Mehmet okula gitti.' (They went together)

rules.title: Turkish Relative Clauses: Don't Use 'ki'
rules.explanation: In Turkish, relative clauses are formed by adding suffixes like -en/-an (for subject) or -dığı/-diği (for object) to the verb, not by using 'ki' or relative pronouns. The clause comes before the noun it modifies.

examples.note: Subject relative: '-en' on verb. 'The man who is coming is my friend.'
Object relative: '-diğim' on verb. 'The book I read is very beautiful.'

exercises.prompt: Translate: The girl who is dancing.
Translate: The car that we saw.
Translate: The teacher who teaches Turkish.

rules.title: Compound Verbs with etmek and olmak
rules.explanation: In Turkish, many compound verbs are formed by pairing a noun or adjective with the auxiliary verbs 'etmek' (to do/make) or 'olmak' (to become/be). A common mistake is using the wrong auxiliary or writing them as one word. Use 'etmek' with nouns (e.g., yardım etmek) and 'olmak' with adjectives (e.g., mutlu olmak). Always write these two words separately.

examples.note: Compound verbs with etmek/olmak are always written as two separate words.
Use 'olmak' with adjectives to mean 'to be/become (adjective)'.
'Teşekkür' is a noun, so it takes 'etmek'.

exercises.prompt: How do you say 'to help' in Turkish?
Which auxiliary verb should be used with the adjective 'mutlu'?
Correct the mistake: 'Teşekkür olmak'.

rules.title: Dropping Subject Pronouns in Turkish
rules.explanation: In Turkish, the verb ending already tells you who the subject is, so subject pronouns like 'ben' (I), 'sen' (you), 'o' (he/she/it) are usually omitted. Keeping them is not grammatically wrong but sounds unnatural and redundant unless you want to emphasize or contrast the subject. Native speakers only use subject pronouns for clarity or emphasis.

examples.note: The verb ending '-um' already means 'I', so 'ben' is unnecessary.
The ending '-sin' indicates 'you', so drop 'sen' in everyday speech.
'O' is only needed if you’re emphasizing who is reading. Normally the verb ending shows third person.

exercises.prompt: Translate 'I am coming' without using a subject pronoun.
Correct this Turkish sentence: 'Ben İstanbul'da yaşıyorum.'
Translate 'You (singular) are very kind' without a subject pronoun.

rules.title: Question Suffix Placement in Turkish
rules.explanation: In Turkish, the question suffix (-mi, -mı, -mu, -mü) attaches to the word or verb stem being questioned and must be followed by the appropriate personal suffix (if any). A common mistake is placing the suffix at the very end of the sentence or omitting the personal suffix after it.

examples.note: With verbs in the present continuous, the question suffix goes between the tense suffix (-iyor) and the personal ending (-sun).
When questioning a noun predicate, add the personal suffix after -mi (e.g., -sin, -sınız).

exercises.prompt: Correct the sentence: 'O kitap okuyor mu?' (intended: 'Is he reading a book?')
exercises.answer: O kitap okuyor mu? (correct as is – but if you meant 'Is he reading a book?' vs 'Is it a book he is reading?', then note: 'O kitap okuyor mu?' is fine for yes/no question. No change needed. Alternative: provide a different prompt.)

exercises.prompt: Translate: 'Are you a student?'
Correct: 'Biz geliyoruz mu?'

rules.title: Numbers and Counters in Turkish
rules.explanation: In Turkish, after a number, the noun always stays singular—never add the plural suffix (-ler/-lar). You can optionally use the counter 'tane' (meaning 'piece') after the number for emphasis, but the noun still remains singular. For example, 'üç kitap' (three books) is correct, while 'üç kitaplar' is wrong.

examples.note: After the number 'beş' (five), 'araba' stays singular.
No plural suffix after numbers; 'elma' remains singular.
Even with the counter 'tane', the noun 'çiçek' does not take a plural suffix.

exercises.prompt: How do you say 'three books' in Turkish? (without using a counter)
Correct this Turkish phrase: 'beş masalar'.
Write 'seven apples' in Turkish using the counter 'tane'.

rules.title: De/Da: Suffix or Separate Word?
rules.explanation: The locative suffix -de/-da is attached directly to a noun to mean 'in' or 'at' (e.g., okulda = at school). The particle de/da is a separate word meaning 'also' or 'too' (e.g., Ali de = Ali too). A common mistake is writing them together or confusing which one to use. Remember: if you mean 'also', write it as a separate word; if you mean a location, attach it to the noun.

examples.note: 'Evde' (at home) is the locative; 'de' (also) is written separately.
'Okulda' (at school) is the locative; 'da' (also) is a separate particle.
'Arkadaşım' (my friend) has no locative; 'da' (also) is a separate word.

exercises.prompt: Write the correct Turkish for 'I am also at home.'
Combine these two sentences using the particle de/da: 'Ali geldi. Ayşe geldi.'
Translate: 'There is also a book in the room.'
